title: Whittled lime spatula
subtitle: A short flat spatula from a seasoned lime blank — first kitchen-tool project
excerpt: A small flat spatula for spreading, lifting, and scraping is one of the simplest functional objects to whittle from a flat seasoned-wood blank. Lime (basswood) is the recommended species: it is the softest and most forgiving of the food-safe hardwoods, carves cleanly with a knife, and is widely available as a carving blank.
sourceNotes: Spatula and utensil forms in domestic craft documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at a stable moisture content. Does not move after carving, making it suitable for tight kitchen-use tolerances.
A carving cut in which the blade travels away from the body, driven by forearm extension.
A short controlled carving cut using the off-hand thumb as a pivot and travel limit.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.

projectSchedule.title: Carve and sand the spatula
projectSchedule.body: Shape the blank, thin the blade edge, sand to 240 grit.

projectSchedule.title: Oil cured: food-safe
projectSchedule.body: Raw linseed oil has cured fully. The spatula is ready for kitchen use.

content.text: A flat wooden spatula is the most useful of the beginner kitchen-tool projects because it is a shape that asks nothing complicated of the maker. Start from a
seasoned-wood
flat
blank

, thin one end toward a flexible-edged blade, round off the corners, sand, and oil. The work is primarily straight-grain push cuts along the flat faces of the blank. Lime (basswood) carves with less resistance than sycamore from a seasoned blank, which makes it forgiving of a knife that is not at its very sharpest and allows a beginner to develop feel for the push cut without fighting the wood.
What you need: wood

A seasoned lime blank, 20 cm long, 5 cm wide, 1.8 cm thick. This is a common size stocked by carving suppliers as a utility blank. Lime carves cleanly with a knife even when fully seasoned, produces no splinters, and dries to a near-white surface that stays pale after oiling. It is food-safe once the oil finish has cured.

Sycamore at the same dimensions is an equally good choice and slightly harder. Beech is harder still but produces a very smooth sanded surface. All three are food-safe and widely available. The spatula made from this tutorial is a short, wide type (good for flipping and lifting) rather than a long thin turner. If a longer spatula is wanted, use a blank of 28 to 30 cm and keep the same 5 cm width.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife with a sharp edge; strop before starting. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grits. Raw linseed oil. The 120 grit is heavier than usual for a first-project sanding start because seasoned lime boards sometimes have a slight roughness from the saw or from the drying; starting coarser saves time. Move to 180 once the surface is even, then 240 to prepare for oiling.
Workspace
A stable seat or low workbench. Good front light. The blank is narrow enough to hold in the hand throughout; no clamping is needed. Keep the strop visible and within arm's reach.
The work
Step 1: Mark the profile

Pencil the outline on the wide face of the blank. The handle takes up the back 8 cm and stays near full width (5 cm) with the corners slightly rounded. The blade begins at the 8 cm point and tapers to about 4.5 cm wide at the tip. The tip itself is square cut with rounded corners, not pointed. This wide-tip shape is the most useful for sliding under food on a pan surface.
Step 2: Thin the blade from both faces
The blade (forward 12 cm) is thinned from the full blank thickness of 1.8 cm down to around 4 to 5 mm at the tip. Work both faces evenly. Use
push cuts

along the flat face, starting at a point 12 cm from the blade tip and angling toward the tip surface. Take thin shavings and check the thickness often by holding the piece up to a light source. The two faces of the blade should thin at the same rate so the blade stays flat and symmetrical.
Step 3: Round the edges and corners
The blade edge (the working end) should be slightly rounded, not knife-sharp. A few
thumb-pivot cuts

along the edge take off the square corners without making the edge too fragile. The handle corners can be taken off the same way to give a comfortable hand feel. The transition between handle and blade, where the thickness changes, should be a smooth slope rather than a sharp step: blend it with a series of light push cuts.
Step 4: Sand

Sand through 120, 180, and 240 grit. Work along the grain on the flat faces. On the curved edges, sand in the direction of the curve. Wipe away dust between grits. The blade section at 240 grit should have a smooth, slightly powdery feel with no visible knife marks. The handle at 240 grit needs to be smooth enough to be comfortable in the hand without surface roughness.
Finishing

Apply raw linseed oil generously and allow to penetrate for one hour. Wipe off all surface oil with a dry cloth. The spatula is not food-safe for two weeks from this point. After two weeks the oil has cured and the spatula is ready for kitchen use. Re-oil whenever the wood looks or feels dry; a spatula in regular use will need re-oiling every three to six months.
Variations
Make the blank narrower (3 cm wide) and longer (30 cm) for a thin turner suitable for crepes and fish fillets. The thinner blade edge is the most demanding part of the thinning work on this variant.
Left-handed variant: the spatula is symmetrical as described and works equally well in either hand. No adjustment needed.

A rounded-nose spatula (elliptical tip rather than square) is slightly more work to shape but presents a more refined finished profile. Pencil the rounded tip on the blank before thinning and round it with a series of push cuts toward the centre before the full thinning pass.
Care
Hand wash in warm water, dry immediately with a towel. Never dishwasher, never soak. The thin blade section in particular will warp if left standing in water. Re-oil when dry.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that come up during the project.

items.symptom: The blade is noticeably thicker on one face than the other
items.cause: More material was removed from one face during thinning.
items.fix: Check thickness with calipers at several points along the blade. Take corrective push cuts on the thicker face, working very lightly near the final dimension.

items.symptom: The wood tears rather than cutting cleanly
items.cause: The cut is going against the grain, or the knife edge is dull.
items.fix: Strop the knife. Try the cut in the opposite direction along the grain; if the surface is smooth, the reverse direction is with the grain.

items.symptom: The blade has a slight twist when viewed along its length
items.cause: Uneven thinning removed more material on one side than the other.
items.fix: Sight along the blade length in good light. Mark the high side and take light corrective passes until the blade sits flat on a surface with no rocking.
